text: These are 13 new discoveries made by AI. Number 13. AI found a new antibiotic against a last resort superbug. Hospitals have been struggling with a pathogen called aena tobactor bon mani. It spreads in ICUs. It infects open wounds and the scary part is that it resists almost every antibiotic doctors normally use. The World Health Organization lists it as a top threat. So researchers used a deep learning model to scan thousands of chemical compounds and predict which ones could slow or stop the bacteria. The AI flagged a molecule scientists later named Abosan. In lab tests, Aosan killed the pathogen with high precision while leaving healthy cells mostly untouched. Traditional drug discovery takes years of trial and error. And in this case, AI cuts the search down to hours. Number 12. AI discovered more than 2500 new ocean microbes and unknown viruses. The ocean is full of DNA from species we've never seen. The problem is that genetic fragments are tiny and mixed together like a shredded book. Humans cannot piece them back together manually. So, a generative AI model was trained to reconstruct full genomes from seawater DNA and it worked. Researchers identified thousands of previously unknown microbes and viruses living deep in ocean ecosystems. Some of them may play a role in storing carbon which affects climate systems and others may eventually be used in medicine or biotechnology. These discoveries did not come from catching the species. They came from AI rebuilding their genetic code from tiny pieces. Number 11. NASA used AI to reveal an ancient river system on Mars. Mars looks dry and empty, but the big question has always been the same. Did water last long enough for life to develop there? NASA trained a machine learning model to study highresolution images and topographic data from the Martian surface. The AI detected hidden branching channels buried underneath layers of dust. These formations match the shape of old river beds on Earth. The data suggests that Mars once had longlasting flowing water, not just melting ice or quick floods. This gives scientists new places to explore as future missions search for signs of past microbial life trapped in Martian rock. Number 10. AI discovered the largest methane leak ever recorded. Methane is one of the strongest greenhouse gases on the planet. And big leaks are hard to catch because they're invisible. Satellite systems connected to AI models can scan the atmosphere and pinpoint where methane's coming from in real time. And one of the largest lakes ever detected showed up as a massive cloud over Turk Menistan along with major leaks in parts of the United States. The total output was estimated to match the emissions of tens of millions of cars. Governments were alerted because the AI did something traditional monitoring could not do quickly. It identified the exact locations which allowed crews to fix the pipelines responsible. Number nine, AI discovered 300 plus hidden Nazca geoglyphs in Peru. The Nazca

to match the emissions of tens of millions of cars. Governments were alerted because the AI did something traditional monitoring could not do quickly. It identified the exact locations which allowed crews to fix the pipelines responsible. Number nine, AI discovered 300 plus hidden Nazca geoglyphs in Peru. The Nazca into the ground, but many of them are too eroded to see with the naked eye. Archaeologists used AI to scan drone and satellite images and look for patterns humans missed. The model detected more than 300 previously unknown geoglyphs, including animals and human-like figures. Many were later confirmed on the ground. Without AI, most of these carvings would have stayed invisible, buried under centuries of wind and dust. It gave researchers a new way to map ancient art that was literally hiding in plain sight. Number eight, AI helped decode the Herculanium scrolls. [music] When Mount Vuvius erupted, it carbonized entire libraries. The scrolls are so fragile that unrolling them would destroy them. Researchers used highresolution CT scans and trained a machine learning model to detect faint ink level signals inside the carbonized layers. The AI successfully reconstructed Greek words and passages that had been unreadable for almost 2,000 years. The method lets historians read texts without touching them. It's opening access to ancient knowledge that's been sealed since the days of the Roman Empire. Number seven, AI found a new type of solar particle interaction. The sun still has mysteries scientists are trying to solve. One of them is why its outer atmosphere, the corona, is hotter than the surface below it. Physicists studying data from the Solar Dynamics Observatory, used AI to analyze wave patterns inside the sun. The model detected previously hidden interactions involving alphen waves. These are magnetic plasma waves that move through the sun's atmosphere. The findings suggest that these wave interactions may be transferring energy and heating the corona. Instead of guessing, scientists finally had direct observational signatures that were too subtle for humans to catch manually. It brings solar researchers one step closer to explaining a decadesl long puzzle. Number six, AI created a new super strong glass-like metal. Materials engineers used AI to design ultraight nano lattises that rival the strength of carbon steel while weighing about as little as foam. Instead of guessing which patterns might work, a model explored thousands of lattice geometries and surfaced designs that spread stress more evenly under load. Researchers then 3D printed the top candidates and found they combined high strength with toughness, a balance that's usually hard to get in nano architected materials. The result is a lightweight structure that stays durable under heavy stress, making it a promising fit for aircraft, spacecraft, and high-performance vehicles where every kilogram matters. It's a clear example of AI discovering architectures humans hadn't tried yet. Number five, AI discovered new crisper enzymes faster than any lab. Gene editing usually depends on crisper enzymes that scientists already know about, but there may be millions more

aircraft, spacecraft, and high-performance vehicles where every kilogram matters. It's a clear example of AI discovering architectures humans hadn't tried yet. Number five, AI discovered new crisper enzymes faster than any lab. Gene editing usually depends on crisper enzymes that scientists already know about, but there may be millions more trained a protein searching model on metagenomic DNA taken from soil and ocean samples. The AI searched through millions of fragments and found entire families of crisper-like proteins. Some of them were smaller and faster than the enzymes commonly used in labs. Smaller enzymes can fit inside cells more easily, which could make gene editing safer and more precise. The discovery did not come from a lab experiment. It came from AI scanning DNA directly from the environment. Number four, AI found the world's oldest known animal tracks. Scientists studying 3D scans of ancient rock surfaces in Namabia worked with computer vision tools to analyze faint fossil impressions. According to research published in Nature Communications, the AI system detected extremely subtle groove and pressure patterns that suggested movement made by early softbodied animals over 500 million years ago. These patterns were difficult for scientists to see with the naked eye, especially after erosion and compression over hundreds of millions of years. The discovery supported the idea that early wormlike creatures were already moving through sediment long before the Cambrian explosion. Instead of manually inspecting thousands of surface images, AI helped reveal details that traditional fossil analysis missed. Number three, AI sequenced ancient human DNA from dirt. Normally, DNA is recovered from bones or teeth, but in some sites, nothing's left. Researchers from Harvard and the Max Plank Institute trained AI to reconstruct genomes from microscopic DNA fragments trapped in soil. The model separated millions of pieces of genetic material and reassembled them into profiles of humans, animals, and plants that lived during the ice age. It helped identify previously unknown populations of early humans along with mammoths and other species. This approach means scientists can study places where fossils are long gone because the DNA in the soil is still there, just broken into pieces that only AI can sort. Number two, AI detected gravitational wave aftershocks no one knew existed. Gravitational waves are tiny ripples in space created by massive events like black hole collisions. The problem is that the signals are so faint that most of the data gets buried in noise. Physicists trained deep learning models on data from detectors like LIGO and found something surprising. After the main gravitational wave signal passed, there were faint echoes that traditional methods never picked up. Some researchers believe these echoes could be caused by distorted spaceime around merging black holes. Others think it might be a new type of physical interaction. It's still being researched, but the detection itself came from AI, not a telescope, which shows how much hidden information is still sitting in old data. Number one, AI predicted dark matter distribution without physics equations. Dark matter

holes. Others think it might be a new type of physical interaction. It's still being researched, but the detection itself came from AI, not a telescope, which shows how much hidden information is still sitting in old data. Number one, AI predicted dark matter distribution without physics equations. Dark matter complex equations and simulations to model where it might be in the universe. A new model was trained directly on telescope data and learned how galaxies and dark matter interact without being told the rules of gravity. It created dark matter maps that matched the results of top physics simulations. It even spotted relationships between galaxies and invisible mass that humans never defined mathematically. Researchers are calling it one of the first examples of AI discovering physical patterns on its own. Instead of replacing physics, it gives scientists new ideas to test. If you made it this far, let us know what you think in the comment section below. And if you haven't seen part one, part two, or part three of this video, make sure you watch them right now. Thanks for watching.

title: Top 13 New Discoveries MADE By AI (Part 4)
